macro_segments.title: The Shomer's Obligation to Swear That a Lost Pikadon Is Not in His Possession

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Ruling: Even a Paying Shomer Must Swear the Object Is Not in His Possession
The Concern: The Shomer May Covet the Object and Claim It Was Lost

macro_segments.title: The Collateral (Mashkon) and the Loan: When Deducting Loss from Debt Becomes Modeh BeKmitzsas

micro_segments.title: Setup: A Lender Holds Collateral That Gets Lost—The Value Deducts from the Loan Owed
Four Scenarios of Mashkon Values vs. Loan Amount—Determining Kofer HaKol vs. Modeh BeKmitzsas
Why the Malveh (Lender) Takes the Oath in Modeh BeKmitzsas Cases: Avoiding False Oaths by Producing the Pikadon
Challenge to Rav Huna: If Malveh Swore He Doesn't Have the Mashkon, Why Fear He'll Produce It?
Rava's Answer: Malveh Swears First That It's Not in His Possession; Then Loveh Swears About Value
Rav Huna Bar Tachalifa Defends Rav Huna: The First Section of the Second Case Challenges Him
Rav Ashi's Explanation: The Loveh Chose Not to Compel the Malveh's Oath—A Case of Trust
Why Trust in Personal Honesty vs. Value Assessment: The Malveh's Economic Advantage
The Theological Turn: Wealthy = Righteous; Poor = Dishonest—Why the Loveh Trusts the Malveh

macro_segments.title: The Case of Lost and Later Found Jewelry: Seizing Property for a Debt and Reversal

micro_segments.title: A Shomer Claims He Lost a Pikadon; Rav Nachman Seizes His House in Payment
The Jewelry Is Found: Should It Revert to the Owner, and Should the House Be Returned?
Rava's Challenge: If the Shomer Bought the Pikadon by Paying, He Owns It and Its Increase
The Distinction: The Shomer Didn't Really Owe a Debt Because the Object Was Never Truly Lost

macro_segments.title: The Doctrine of Shuma Hadar: Can Assessed Property in Payment of Debt Be Redeemed?

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Shuma Hadar: When a Creditor Seizes a Debtor's Property, Can the Debtor Later Redeem It?
The Nardaei Opinion: Redemption Allowed for Twelve Months; Amemar: Always Allowed
The Halakha: Shuma Hadar LeOlam—Ethical Obligation of Ve'Asita HaYashar VeHaTov
Redemption Through Chains of Creditors: Each Successive Creditor Must Allow Redemption
Exception: Sale, Gift, or Inheritance—When Property Leaves the Money Economy and Ve'Asita Doesn't Apply
The Husband's Status Upon Wife's Death: Purchaser, Not Heir—Cannot Revert Property
Voluntary Seizure vs. Forced Seizure: Rav Acha and Ravina Dispute Whether Willing Transfer Is a True Sale
When Does a Creditor Own Seized Property: Writ, Signature, or End of Auction Period?

topical_tags.term: Rav Huna's ruling (shomer must swear not in possession)
Gilgul Shbua (adding oath to existing oath)
Ve'Asita HaYashar VeHaTov (ethical obligation to do what is right)
Rav Acha and Ravina (disputants on voluntary seizure)
Rava (interpreter and challenger of earlier positions)
